title: AI Agents
body:
Overview

Every FarmClaw instance comes with at least one AI agent — your farm's assistant. Out of the box, a single general-purpose agent handles grain bids, weather, scale tickets, and anything else you ask about.

But FarmClaw also supports multiple agents per instance. Each agent can have its own personality, its own skill set, and its own Discord channel assignment. Think of it as building a small team of specialists for your operation.

Single agent vs. multi-agent

Single agent (the defau

title: Multi-Agent Setups
body:
Overview

FarmClaw offers three pre-built multi-agent configurations that you can deploy with one click. Each setup is designed around real farm workflows — you pick the configuration that matches your operation's needs, and FarmClaw handles the rest.

You can also build a custom setup from scratch, but these suggested configurations are a good starting point for most operations.

Suggested setups

Two-Agent: General + Grain

An all-purpose assistant for daily operations and a specialist for gra

title: Personality Presets
body:
Overview

Every FarmClaw agent has a personality preset that defines how it communicates, what it focuses on, and what it will and won't do. Think of presets as job descriptions for your AI assistants — they set the tone, expertise, and boundaries.

FarmClaw ships with five built-in presets plus a custom option. Each preset is carefully designed for its role, including explicit guardrails about topics the agent will not handle.

Available presets

General Advisor

The all-purpose farm assistant.

title: Skills & Scheduling

headings.title: Three-agent setup: General + Grain + Scale Tickets
Example: setting up a three-agent system

body:
Skills & Scheduling

Each agent in your FarmClaw instance can be assigned a specific subset of skills and its own set of scheduled cron jobs. This lets you create focused specialists -- a Grain Desk agent that only handles bids, a Weather Watch agent that only handles forecasts, or a Ticket Clerk that only logs deliveries.

How per-agent skills work

FarmClaw has two levels of skill configuration:

1. Global skill toggle -- Enables or disables a skill for your entire instance (Settings > Skills)

title: Billing & Usage

headings.title: What's included in the base subscription

body:
How billing works

FarmClaw uses a straightforward pricing model with two components:

1. Monthly base subscription — A fixed $40/month fee that covers your dedicated cloud instance, container hosting, and platform access
2. Metered API usage — Billed at cost based on the actual AI model tokens your assistant consumes

This structure keeps the base price low while ensuring you only pay for what you use.

What's included in the base subscription

Your $40/month subscription includes:

- A dedicat

title: Metered Usage
body:
How metering works

Every interaction with your AI assistant consumes tokens — the unit of measurement for AI model usage. FarmClaw tracks token usage daily and bills it as a separate line item on top of your $40/month subscription.

What are tokens?

Tokens are the building blocks of AI text processing. Roughly:

- 1 token ≈ 4 characters of English text
- 100 tokens ≈ 75 words
- A typical question and answer uses 500–2,000 tokens

Both the message you send and the response your assistant genera

title: Subscription
body:
Plan details

FarmClaw offers a single subscription plan:

| Feature | Details |
|---------|---------|
| Monthly price | $40.00 |
| Billing cycle | Monthly |
| Payment processor | Stripe |
| Cancellation | Anytime, effective end of billing period |

What you get

Your subscription activates a dedicated FarmClaw instance that includes:

- Dedicated cloud server — Your own VPS running 24/7
- Three built-in skills — Grain bids, weather forecasts, scale tickets
- Multi-agent support — Up to 4 specia

title: Channel Configuration
body:
Overview

Channel configuration lets you control which AI agent responds in which Discord channel. This is how you keep conversations organized — grain questions go to the grain expert, weather questions go to the weather agent, and your general assistant handles everything else.

If you're running a single agent (the default), it responds in all channels and DMs. Channel bindings only matter when you have multiple agents set up.

How channel bindings work

Each agent in your FarmClaw instance c

title: Discord Integration
body:
Why Discord?

FarmClaw delivers your AI assistant through Discord because it's the best fit for how farmers actually work:

- Mobile-first — Discord runs on your phone, tablet, and desktop. Ask for grain bids from the combine cab or check spray windows from the pickup.
- Already familiar — Many farmers and farm families already use Discord. No new app to learn.
- Multi-channel — Organize conversations by topic. Put grain talk in one channel, weather in another, scale tickets in a third.
- Thread

body:
Overview

This guide walks you through creating a Discord server, setting up a bot application, and connecting it to FarmClaw. The entire process takes about 10 minutes.

You'll need:

- A Discord account (free at discord.com)
- A web browser for the Discord Developer Portal
- Access to your FarmClaw onboarding wizard or Settings page

Step 1: Create a Discord server

If you already have a Discord server for your farm, skip to Step 2.

1. Open Discord (desktop app or web)
2. Click the + button i

title: Extensions
body:
Extensions

The FarmClaw extension system lets you expand your AI assistant's capabilities beyond the three built-in skills. Extensions are installable packages that add new skills, plugins, connectors, MCP servers, and tool packs to your instance.

Extension types

Extensions come in five types, each serving a different purpose:

| Type | What it does | Example |
|---|---|---|
| skill | Adds a new capability or workflow to your assistant | A livestock price skill, a soil sampling analyzer |
| p

title: Installing Extensions
body:
Installing Extensions

This guide covers the complete extension installation workflow: configuring your trust policy, installing extensions from the catalog, monitoring health, and understanding the staged rollout system for updates.

Trust policies

Every FarmClaw account has an extension trust policy that controls which extensions can be installed. The trust policy has two components:

Allowed source types

The allowedsourcetypes list determines which source types you permit at all. By default

title: Marketplace
body:
Marketplace

The FarmClaw extension marketplace is the catalog of all available extensions you can add to your AI assistant. Browse by type, filter by source, and check verification status before installing.

Browsing the catalog

The extension catalog is accessible from Settings > Extensions in your FarmClaw dashboard. The Catalog section at the bottom of the page displays all active extensions with their key details.

Each extension entry in the catalog shows:

- Display name -- The human-read

title: Crop Selection

headings.title: How Crop Selection Affects Your Experience

body:
Crop Selection

FarmClaw supports 10 row crops commonly grown in the Upper Midwest. During onboarding, you select the crops you grow as part of your farm profile. You must select at least one crop, and you can select as many as apply to your operation.

Supported Crops and Bushel Weights

Each crop has a standard bushel weight used for weight-to-bushel conversions throughout the platform. These weights follow USDA standard bushel weights and are used whenever your AI assistant converts between p

title: Elevator Configuration

headings.title: How the Grain Bids Skill Uses Your Elevators

body:
Elevator Configuration

Elevator configuration connects your FarmClaw assistant to the grain elevators you deliver to. Once configured, your assistant can pull real-time cash grain bids from each elevator's website, compare prices across locations, and help you decide where to haul.

You set up elevators during Step 2 of onboarding (Grain Marketing) and can update them at any time from your dashboard.

What Each Elevator Needs

Every elevator in your configuration has three pieces of information

title: Farm Profile
body:
Farm Profile

Your farm profile is the first thing you fill out during onboarding. It tells FarmClaw where you are, what you grow, and the size of your operation. Every other feature -- grain bids, weather, scale tickets, agent behavior -- builds on this information.

Required Fields

All fields in the farm profile are required. Here is what each one does and what the system expects.

Farm Name

Your farm or operation name. This is used throughout the dashboard and in your AI assistant's configu

title: Farm Setup
body:
Farm Setup

Before your FarmClaw AI assistant can start helping you, it needs to know the basics about your operation. Farm setup is the first step in onboarding and takes about five minutes. The information you provide here drives everything your assistant does -- from pulling the right grain bids to calculating bushel weights on scale tickets.

Farm setup covers three key areas:

Farm Profile

Your farm profile includes your farm name, state, county, crop selection, and total acreage. This is

title: Account Setup
body:
Magic link authentication

FarmClaw uses magic link authentication powered by Supabase Auth. There are no passwords to remember or manage.

How it works

1. Enter your email address on the login or signup page
2. Check your inbox for an email from FarmClaw
3. Click the login link in the email
4. You're signed in — the link is single-use and expires after a short time

Your session stays active as you use FarmClaw. The system automatically refreshes your session in the background, so you won't be

title: Welcome to FarmClaw
body:
What is FarmClaw?

FarmClaw gives your farm a dedicated AI assistant that lives in your Discord server. Ask it for cash grain bids, weather forecasts, spray window assessments, and more — all in plain English, right from your phone or desktop.

Each FarmClaw instance is a fully configured multi-agent system powered by Claude, Anthropic's AI model. You get real-time access to the information that matters during the growing season and harvest.

Who is it for?

FarmClaw is designed for row crop far

title: Quick Start
body:
Overview

This guide walks you through the entire setup process. By the end, you'll have a working AI assistant in your Discord server that can fetch grain bids, check weather, and log scale tickets.

Step 1: Create your account

Go to the FarmClaw signup page and enter your email address. We use magic link authentication — no password required. Check your inbox for the login link and click it to access your dashboard.

Magic link emails occasionally land in spam or promotions folders. The email

title: Connecting Services

headings.title: Dashboard did not update after completing OAuth
Revoking access from the service side

body:
Connecting Services

Each toolkit that requires authentication needs a one-time OAuth connection before your assistant can use it. This page covers the full connection lifecycle -- authorizing, monitoring, refreshing, and disconnecting services.

The OAuth flow

OAuth is the industry-standard protocol for granting third-party access to your accounts without sharing passwords. When you connect a service through FarmClaw, you go through the service provider's own login and consent screens. FarmCla

title: Integrations

headings.title: What can your assistant do with integrations?

body:
Integrations

Integrations let your AI assistant connect to external services like Google Sheets, Gmail, Google Calendar, Slack, and hundreds more. Instead of being limited to skills and local tools, your assistant can read spreadsheets, send emails, create calendar events, and interact with the services you already use on the farm.

FarmClaw integrations are powered by Composio, a platform that provides secure, authenticated access to 800+ third-party APIs through a single connection. Composio

title: Security and Privacy

headings.title: When your assistant uses an integration
Can FarmClaw employees access my Google Sheets / emails / calendar?
What happens to my data if I cancel my FarmClaw subscription?
Can I use integrations without giving FarmClaw access to my email?

body:
Security and Privacy

This page explains how FarmClaw's Composio integration handles your credentials, what data flows between systems, and what controls you have over access.

Architecture overview

Three systems are involved in the integrations flow:

| System | What it stores | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| FarmClaw | Composio entity ID, MCP server URL, toolkit selections, cached connection status | Manages configuration, triggers config rebuilds, serves the dashboard UI |
| Composio | OAuth

Prerequisites

Before you can enable integrations, you need:

- An active FarmClaw subscription

If your instance is not yet provisioned, complete the onboarding wizard first.

Step 1: Enable Composio

1. Navigate to Settings > Integrations in your FarmC

Available Toolkits

FarmClaw curates a set of Composio toolkits with descriptions and recommendations tailored to Upper Midwest row crop farming. Each toolkit bundles the most useful actions for a single service.

Recommended toolkits

These four toolkits cover the most common integration use cases for farmers. They are highlighted in the Recommended tab when selecting toolkits.

Google Sheets

title: Filesystem Server
body:
Filesystem Server

The Filesystem MCP server gives your AI assistant the ability to read, write, and manage files within its workspace directory. This is useful for saving reports, keeping notes, storing exported data, and working with files that persist across conversations.

Overview

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Package | @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem |
| Command | npx |
| Arguments | -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem /home/node/workspace |
| Scope | Instance (available to

title: MCP Servers

headings.title: How MCP servers fit into your instance

body:
MCP Servers

Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers extend your AI assistant with additional tools and data access beyond its default capabilities. Each MCP server runs as a sidecar process alongside your AI instance, giving the assistant structured access to filesystems, databases, persistent memory, and more.

What is MCP?

MCP is an open protocol that lets AI assistants connect to external tool servers using a standardized interface. Instead of building tool access directly into the AI model, M

title: SQLite Server
body:
SQLite Server

The SQLite MCP server gives your AI assistant access to a local SQLite database, enabling it to store, query, and manage structured data. It is the backbone of the Scale Tickets skill and can also be used for any custom data storage you need.

Overview

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Package | @modelcontextprotocol/server-sqlite |
| Command | npx |
| Arguments | -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-sqlite /home/node/data/farmclaw.db |
| Scope | Instance (available to all agents) |
| Dat

title: Grain Bids
body:
Grain Bids

The Grain Bids skill scrapes your configured elevator websites using a browser tool to extract currently posted cash grain bid information. It fetches live data every time you ask -- no cached or stale prices.

How it works

When you ask about grain prices, the assistant:

1. Opens each configured elevator's cash bid webpage in a headless browser.
2. Takes an ARIA snapshot of the page to understand its structure.
3. Locates the cash bid table and extracts rows matching your configure

title: Skills
body:
Skills

Skills define what your AI assistant can actually do. Each skill provides a set of instructions, tools, and workflows that enable your assistant to perform specific farm tasks -- from fetching grain bids off elevator websites to reading scale tickets from a photo.

Built-in skills

Every FarmClaw instance ships with three built-in skills. These cover the core tasks that Upper Midwest row crop farmers use daily.

| Skill | What it does | Key capability |
|---|---|---|
| Grain Bids | Scrap

title: Scale Tickets
body:
Scale Tickets

The Scale Tickets skill lets you log, track, query, and export grain delivery records directly from Discord. It supports three entry methods -- snap a photo of the ticket, dictate the details by voice or text, or walk through a guided entry flow. All data is stored in a local SQLite database on your instance.

How it works

Scale ticket data is stored in a SQLite database managed through an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server. When you enable the Scale Tickets skill, FarmClaw auto

title: Weather Forecasts
body:
Weather Forecasts

The Weather Forecasts skill provides agriculture-focused weather intelligence powered by the National Weather Service (NWS) API. It goes beyond standard forecasts with spray window analysis, Growing Degree Day (GDD) tracking, and automatic morning briefings -- all calibrated for Upper Midwest row crop farming.

Data source

All weather data comes from the National Weather Service API (api.weather.gov). This is a free, reliable, public API maintained by NOAA. No API key is requ

title: Tools
body:
Tools

FarmClaw tools are delivered through skills. Each skill gives your assistant a focused capability and the workflow needed to execute it reliably.

Tool inventory

The current built-in tool set includes three skills:

| Tool | Skill ID | What it does | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grain Bids | farm-grain-bids | Visits your configured elevator pages and extracts current cash bids. | Price checks, cross-elevator comparisons, and finding the best bid by commodity |
| Weather Forecasts | far

title: Workflow KPI Review Cadence
body:
Workflow KPI Review Cadence

Run a weekly launch review every Monday at 16:00 UTC.

Required KPIs

- Time to first publish (median)
- Draft to publish completion rate
- Run success rate
- Retry rate
- Retry recovery success rate
- Approval cycle time (p50)
- Weekly active builders
- Estimated operator hours saved

Meeting agenda

1. Check KPI trend deltas week-over-week.
2. Review performance gates and sample sufficiency.
3. Decide rollout action: promote, hold, or rollback ring.
4. Assign follo

title: Workflow Builder Rollout Rings
body:
Workflow Builder Rollout Rings

FarmClaw uses three rollout rings to release workflow builder updates safely:

- Canary: first 10% of customers by deterministic customer-ID bucket.
- Standard: next 60% of customers.
- Delayed: remaining 30% of customers after gates pass.

Target ring control

The environment variable WORKFLOWBUILDERROLLOUTTARGETRING controls how far release is opened:

- canary: canary customers only.
- standard: canary + standard.
- delayed: all rings.

If unset or invalid, Far

title: Visual Workflow Builder
body:
Visual Workflow Builder

Use FarmClaw's visual workflow builder to design automations without calling APIs.

Guided walkthrough

1. Open Settings > Workflows (/settings/workflows).
2. Select New workflow and choose a starter template.
3. Add or edit nodes in the canvas, then resolve validation errors in the right panel.
4. Click Publish to promote the current draft and trigger runtime deploy.
5. Click Run or open Runs to verify execution and artifacts.
6. Open Launch readiness to review rollout

headings.title: I didn't receive the magic link email

I'm getting redirected to the login page repeatedly
The bot appears offline in Discord
The bot is online but not responding
Photo OCR didn't read my ticket correctly
Can't find a ticket I logged
I was charged but my instance isn't working
How do I get a refund?

body:
Login & authentication

I didn't receive the magic link email

- Check your spam, junk, or promotions folder
- Verify you entered the correct email address
- Wait 60 seconds before requesting another link — there's a cooldown
- If using a corporate email, your IT department may be blocking the sender
- Try a different email provider (Gmail, Outlook) if issues persist

The magic link says it's expired

Magic links expire after a short time for security. Request a new one from the login page. Don'

title: Troubleshooting
body:
Getting help

If you run into issues with FarmClaw, this section covers the most common problems and their solutions. For issues not covered here, contact support through your dashboard.

Quick diagnostics

Before diving into specific issues, check these basics:

1. Instance status — Check your dashboard. Your instance should show "Running" status.
2. Discord bot — Verify the bot appears online (green dot) in your Discord server's member list.
3. Subscription — Confirm your subscription is activ

title: Workflow Builder Support Runbook
body:
Workflow Builder Support Runbook

Use this runbook when workflow builder behavior impacts operators or production runs.

Severity model

- P1: publish failures or run failures across multiple customers with active impact.
- P2: single-customer publish/run failures that block operations.
- P3: degraded UX with available workaround.

First-response checklist (first 15 minutes)

1. Confirm incident scope: customer IDs, instance IDs, workflow IDs, run IDs.
2. Check /settings/workflows/launch for KPI
